meta.title: ACD score — 2-year mortality after status epilepticus
meta.authors: Roberg LE, Monsson O, Kristensen SB, et al., Beier CP
meta.outcome: Two-year mortality after an episode of non-anoxic status epilepticus, from the Age–Consciousness–Duration (ACD) score (Roberg 2022 nomogram).
meta.source_note: ACD score and 2-year-mortality nomogram: Roberg et al., JAMA Neurol 2022 (doi 10.1001/jamaneurol.2022.0609, PMID 35404392). Independently validated for long-term mortality by Alanís-Bernal et al., Epilepsia 2026 (doi 10.1002/epi.70363, PMID 42396999).
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. The ACD score (Age, Consciousness, Duration) estimates 2-year mortality after an episode of non-anoxic status epilepticus (SE). It was derived and internally-externally validated by Roberg et al. (JAMA Neurology 2022) in Danish, German and Norwegian cohorts, and the 2-year mortality figures shown here are read directly from their published nomogram (Figure 2C).

How it is scored. Three items are added — age at diagnosis (0–6), duration of SE (0–7) and level of consciousness at admission (0–2) — for a total of 0–15. Two-year mortality rises almost linearly with the total (about 6% per point). The optimal cut-off is 10 points: at or above it, more than half of patients have died by 2 years.

Recent external validation. Alanís-Bernal et al. (Epilepsia 2026) confirmed the ACD score as an independent predictor of longer-term (5-year) mortality (HR 1.16 per point, 95% CI 1.06–1.27; ROC area 0.72), with survival again falling below 50% at 2 years once the score reached ≥10.

Scope. Adults / adolescents (≥16–18 years) with non-anoxic SE; the score is most reliable in patients whose SE was not damaging or less damaging to the brain (it under-performs in progressive or brain-damaging causes). Values for scores 0–1 and 15 lie just outside the published nomogram range (2–14) and are extrapolated along the same regression line. Population-level estimates — always apply clinical judgement.

predictors.name: Age at diagnosis
predictors.assess: Patient age when status epilepticus was diagnosed. Older age scores more (0–6 points).

predictors.name: Duration of status epilepticus
predictors.assess: Total duration of the SE episode, from clinical onset to cessation of ictal activity. Longer episodes score more (0–7 points).

predictors.name: Level of consciousness at admission
predictors.assess: Level of consciousness on admission. Stupor or coma adds 2 points; an awake or somnolent patient adds none.

model.note: Two-year mortality from the Roberg (2022) ACD nomogram — about 6% per point; ≥10 points means >50% 2-year mortality. Scores 0–1 and 15 are extrapolated just beyond the published nomogram range (2–14). Estimates apply to non-anoxic SE and are most reliable in non–brain-damaging causes.
model.viz_info: Age, duration and consciousness each add points; the waterfall shows the build-up. The total maps to the predicted 2-year mortality (Roberg nomogram) shown in the donut — roughly 6% per point.

provenance.source: Point table from Roberg et al., JAMA Neurol 2022, Figure 1C (identical to Alanís-Bernal 2026 Fig 3B). Per-score 2-year mortality read from the Roberg Figure 2C nomogram (Total points 2–14 → 13,19,25,31,37,43,49,55,61,67,73,79,86 %; a linear ruler, mortality ≈ 6·score + 1). Scores 0, 1, 15 extrapolated along the same regression line (Fig 2B, Pearson r²=0.848). External long-term validation: Alanís-Bernal et al., Epilepsia 2026 (doi 10.1002/epi.70363, PMID 42396999).
provenance.notes: Age single 4-level bracket (≤40/41-60/61-80/>80→0/2/4/6); Duration 8-level (0-7); Consciousness 2-level (0/2). Max 15. pct = 2-year mortality per Roberg Fig 2C nomogram (exact for scores 2-14; 0/1/15 extrapolated on the published line). Figure-read but the mapping is an explicit linear ruler, so effectively exact. Roberg reports it in survival terms; shown here as mortality (=100−survival) per user request.
